ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general omelette class; no specific onion omelette entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers omelette as a generic egg dish. No onion-specific variant code exists; closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for onion omelette; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of onion omelette is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a 2–3 egg omelette with onion filling as typically served at a hawker stall or casual Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg, whole, cooked, fried (FDC ID 173424) and Onions, raw (FDC ID 170000); values adapted for onion omelette composition
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for onion omelette exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FDC fried egg (FDC ID 173424) as the primary base, blended with USDA raw onion (FDC ID 170000) at approximately 20% onion by weight, and adjusted for fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning. Input record values (energy 190 kcal, protein 9 g, fat 15 g, carbohydrate 5 g, sodium 425 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this composition and are retained. Confidence is medium due to estimation from component analogy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on egg-based dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for onion omelette. Egg-based dishes are consistently low-GI foods due to negligible carbohydrate content and high protein and fat content which slow gastric emptying. A conservative GI of 50 (low category) is assigned by analogy with egg-based dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). At a 200 g serving providing 10 g total carbohydrate, glycemic load = (50 × 10) / 100 = 5.0, which is low. This is a conservative upper-bound estimate; the true GI may be lower given the dominant protein and fat matrix.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold, driven by fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per meal, request reduced or omitted fish sauce, and choose low-sodium soy sauce alternatives to bring sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g) and low sugar (2 g/100 g); egg-based dish with low glycemic index and negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Onion omelette is suitable for blood glucose management due to its low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load (GL 5 per 200 g serving); however, individuals with concurrent dyslipidaemia should moderate egg yolk frequency given the cholesterol content of 225 mg per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 225 mg per 100 g and sodium 425 mg per 100 g; saturated fat 3.5 g per 100 g is below the 5 g threshold but contributes to overall lipid load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100 g or less), avoid added salt or high-sodium condiments, and balance egg intake within the recommended weekly dietary cholesterol budget of no more than 300 mg per day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.5 g/100 g is below the 5 g threshold; onions provide quercetin with anti-inflammatory properties, but no strong omega-3 or dominant anti-inflammatory factor is present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Onion omelette is acceptable in moderation for RA patients; use fresh vegetable oil and avoid reused or overheated oil to minimise pro-inflammatory oxidation products, and monitor lipid profile if on corticosteroids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion Omelette (洋葱煎蛋; Thai: ไข่เจียวหัวหอม) is a pan-fried egg dish popular across Southeast Asia, particularly in Thai and Singaporean hawker cuisine. It is prepared by beating whole eggs with sliced or diced onions, seasoning with fish sauce and soy sauce, then frying in vegetable oil over medium-high heat until the exterior is golden and slightly crispy while the interior remains soft and fluffy. The dish has a savoury, mildly sweet flavour from the caramelised onions, with a rich egg aroma and a tender, slightly custardy texture. Per 100 g it provides approximately 190 kcal, 9 g protein, 15 g fat (3.5 g saturated), 5 g carbohydrate, 2 g sugar, 0.75 g dietary fibre, and 425 mg sodium. Cholesterol is elevated at 225 mg per 100 g due to whole egg yolks. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 50) and a low glycemic load (GL 5 per 200 g serving), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and cholesterol content. It is halal-certified in most hawker contexts. A standard serving is one plate of approximately 200 g, providing around 380 kcal. Onion omelette is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice or as a light standalone meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion omelette?
Is onion omelette suitable for diabetics?
Can hypertension patients eat onion omelette?
Is onion omelette high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for onion omelette exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC fried egg and raw onion component data, consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate from egg-based dish literature. Confidence is medium due to component-level estimation rather than a direct food composition match.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central: fried egg (FDC ID 173424) as primary base (~80% by weight) and raw onion (FDC ID 170000) (~20% by weight), adjusted for fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning. Input record values are consistent with this composition and retained. Monounsaturated fat (5.8 g) and polyunsaturated fat (3.2 g) estimated from USDA fried egg fatty acid profile; these are approximate. Vitamin B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific Thai-style onion omelette preparation not available as a composite dish entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. Potassium estimated at 150 mg/100 g from component analogy. Vitamin A estimated at 70 mcg RAE from egg yolk contribution. Glycemic index set to 50 (low category) as a conservative analogous estimate for an egg-based dish; glycemic load per 200 g serving = 5.0 (low). The dish is listed as halal in the input record and is consistent with halal preparation using permissible ingredients. Country of origin noted as Singapore in input record; primary cuisine is Thai, reflecting the cooking style. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' in the input record appear to be erroneous menu-context artefacts and have not been carried forward. Iteration 3 fix: glycemic_index changed from null to numeric value 50 to satisfy q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category updated from 'not_applicable' to 'low'; gi_source updated to reference peer-reviewed analogous estimate basis.
